segments.s698-g9: چو here is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. خراد برزین is a personal name (two elements); شنید is a past tense verb 'heard' with the subject omitted (he). این سخن = 'this speech/these words' is the direct object. Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal clause (چو ...) + verb.
segments.s698-g10: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. بر here marks direction/target 'to/toward' (he came to). آن جایگاه کهن = 'that ancient seat/place' (آن determiner + noun جایگاه modified by adjective کهن).
segments.s698-g11: بدیدش is past 'saw' with the object suffix -ش ('saw it' / 'he saw it'); یکی = 'a certain / one'; جای کرده بلند is a noun phrase where جای 'seat/place' is modified by the participial/compound کرده بلند 'made high' (کرده = past participle 'made', بلند = 'high/lofty'). The phrase describes a single lofty-made seat.
segments.s698-g12: سوار ایستاده = 'a rider standing' (ایستاده is a participle/adjective after the noun). در او = 'in it' where در = 'in' and او is the demonstrative/pronoun referring back to the seat; ارجمند = 'esteemed/noble' qualifying the rider. The clause order is noun + participle + locative phrase + adjective.
segments.s698-g13: کجا here is rhetorical 'where/ever' meaning 'nowhere/never' in this negative context. چشم بیننده = 'the eye of the beholder' (compound noun + agentive). چونان ندید = 'saw not such / had not seen such' — ندید is past negative 'did not see' (ن- prefix + دید). The whole is a rhetorical question/negation: 'Where has the beholder's eye seen such?'
segments.s698-g14: بدان سان is an adverbial phrase 'in that manner' (ب- + دان/آن + سان = 'by/with that manner'); توگفتی is second-person past 'you said' introducing reported speech; خدای آفرید is the quoted clause 'God created' (آفرید = past 'created'). Together: 'You said, "God created (him) so."' Note دان as listed senses does not correspond to a verbal sense here but functions as part of the demonstrative compound.
segments.s698-g15: بدید = 'he saw' (past). ایستاده معلق سوار is a sequence of modifiers: ایستاده 'standing' and معلق 'suspended/hovering' both qualify سوار 'rider', producing 'a rider standing and suspended' (poetic juxtaposition of participles).
segments.s698-g16: بیامد = 'came' (past). بر = directional 'to/toward' (he approached); قیصر نامدار = 'the famed Caesar' (قیصر 'Caesar/emperor', نامدار 'renowned'). The clause states that he came up to the famous emperor.
segments.s698-g105: بروبوم (burbūm) 'homeland' appears first as the thing being affected; دارد is the present-tense/possessive verb 'has' (here used idiomatically). ز دشمن نگاه is a prepositional phrase 'a gaze from the enemy' that complements دارد, so the whole line literally says 'he has (/there is) a gaze from the enemy on the homeland' — a poetic inversion that means 'the enemy looks upon (or keeps an eye on) the homeland.'
segments.s698-g106: جز این را is a fronted restriction 'nothing except this' with را used as the definite/emphatic object marker; نخواهد is the classical third-person verb from خواستن used here in the sense 'will want/desire' (i.e. 'wants nothing but...'). خردمند شاه is inverted adjective+noun order ('the wise king') serving as the subject — classical Persian often places the adjective before the noun.
segments.s698-g107: جز از راستی 'except from truth' sets the exclusion; هرک is an archaic form of هر که 'whoever' and is the indefinite subject; جوید is the classical present-tense third-person singular of جستن 'to seek' ('seeks'); ز دین 'from/of religion' is the source phrase attached to جوید. The word order is poetic but maps to 'whoever seeks of religion other than truth.'
segments.s698-g108: بر او باد is a fixed optative formula: باد here is the optative particle 'may it be' rather than the noun 'wind', so بر او باد X = 'may X be upon him.' نفرین بی‌آفرین is the noun 'a curse' qualified by بی‌آفرین 'unpraiseworthy' (an epithet); together they express a wish/curse directed at the aforedescribed person.
segments.s698-g109: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' and appears before the subject (قيصر) due to poetic inversion; پسند آمدش is an idiom where پسند 'approval/pleasing' plus آمدش 'came to him' means 'it pleased him' or 'he liked it.' The clause thus reads 'When Caesar heard, it pleased him.'
segments.s698-g110: سخن‌های او is the subject 'his words/speeches' (سخن‌های is the plural). سودمند آمدش again uses آمدش 'came to him' in the experiential sense 'seemed/appeared to him,' so 'his words appeared useful to him' or 'he found his words beneficial.' The adjective precedes آمدش, a common poetic predicate structure.
segments.s698-g111: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' with بدو = 'to him' (prepositional pronoun) and گفت the past-tense verb 'said.' آن‌کو is an archaic relative pronoun (آن که) meaning 'he who' or 'the one who,' followed by جهان آفرید 'created the world' (آفرید is past tense 'created'). The relative clause describes 'the one who created the world.'
segments.s698-g112: تو را places the direct object 'you' before the verb, with را as the definite/emphatic object marker. نامدار is an adjective/noun 'renowned' and مهان is a poetic plural referring to 'the great/important ones' (here the group among whom the addressee is made famous). آفرید is the past tense 'made/created.' The clause thus states that the creator made you renowned among the great.
segments.s698-g113: This is an impersonal/agentless construction: سخن‌های پاک (plural noun + adjective) is the theme 'pure words', followed by the source phrase از تو 'from you', and the modal-impersonal باید شنید 'one must hear'. In Persian the verb (باید شنید) comes at the end; باید is the necessity modal and شنید is the infinitive/impersonal form 'to hear.'
segments.s698-g114: تو داری is the present-tense, personal verb 'you have/possess'. در here is the noun 'door/diadem' (poetic use) forming a genitive with رازها ('the door/vestibule of secrets'), and را is the definite-object marker attached in the line's word order to mark کلید as the definite object 'the key.' The sentence thus says you possess the (figurative) key of the door of secrets.
segments.s698-g115: کسی را uses the indefinite/particular 'someone' with the object/particular marker را (a classical idiom often introducing an existential/particular clause). کز is the relative particle 'who/that', and the clause این‌گونه کهتر بود is the predicate 'was inferior/less of such a kind'—کهتر is a comparative/adjectival predicate and بود is the past copula.
segments.s698-g116: سرش is the possessive 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش). ز is the poetic comparative preposition 'than/from' introducing the standard of comparison, افسر ماه is a genitive phrase 'the diadem/helmet of the moon', برتر is the comparative 'higher/superior', and بود is the past copula 'was.' The whole clause means 'his head was higher than the moon's diadem.'
segments.s698-g117: This line repeats خواست for metrical and emphatic effect. درم خواست is 'he asked for dirhams'; از گنج و دینار خواست coordinately lists further requested items 'he asked for treasure and dinars.' از marks the source/objects requested, and the verbs are past-tense خواست.
segments.s698-g118: یکی here functions as 'a certain' (indefinite/determinate) preceding the noun افسری 'a diadem/helmet' and the adjective نامبردار 'renowned/celebrated' modifies it; خواست is the past verb 'he requested/wanted.' Word order is noun phrase + verb as usual in Persian.
segments.s698-g119: بدو داد 'he gave to him' uses بدو (to him/unto it) as the indirect object followed by the past verb داد 'gave'. و بسیار کرد آفرین is a coordinated clause: بسیار is an adverb 'much/very', کرد is the light verb 'did/made', and آفرین here is a noun/nominalized acclamation 'praise'—together 'he lavished much praise (on him)'.
segments.s698-g120: که begins the content clause 'that/so that'; آباد is an adjective 'prosperous/inhabited'; باد is the optative particle 'may' (wishing form). از تو marks the cause/agent 'because of you' and ایران‌زمین is the subject 'the land of Iran.' Together the clause is an optative blessing: 'May the land of Iran be prosperous because of you.'
segments.s698-g81: بخندد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive form used poetically as a general statement ('laughs' / 'let him laugh'). بر این is the prepositional object 'at this' (بخندد بر این = 'laughs at this'). The final phrase بر خردمند مرد is a poetic inversion (adjective before noun) meaning 'the wise man' (مرد = man/person), so the whole segment reads 'a wise man laughs at this.'
segments.s698-g82: تو‌گر = تو گر is an archaic/poetic conditional 'if you'. بخردی here functions as a predicative idea ('are wise' / 'have wisdom') rather than a simple noun. گرد این فن is 'around/about this art' with گرد used as a verbal/relational element, and مگرد is a prohibitive/negative form ('do not turn/approach' — 'do not meddle'). Together: 'If you are wise, do not busy yourself with this art.'
segments.s698-g83: که here introduces an explanatory clause ('for/since'). هست is the copula 'is/exists.' او 'he' is the subject. ز (poetic for از) means 'from/of' and combines with فرزند و زن 'children and wife' to yield 'free from need of children and a wife.' بی‌نیاز is the predicate adjective 'without need/self-sufficient.'
segments.s698-g84: به نزدیک او places the scene 'near him' (به + نزدیک = 'at/in the nearness of'). آشکار است is a copular clause 'is manifest/clear.' راز 'secret' is the predicate noun. Word order is standard: locative phrase + predicate, yielding 'the secret is revealed near him.'
segments.s698-g85: چه پیچی is an exclamatory phrase 'what a twist/departure' (چه = exclamative). ز دین کیومرثی literally 'from the religion/faith of Keyumars' — ز = 'from' (poetic form of از), دین = 'religion/faith', کیومرثی = 'of Keyumars'. The segment laments a turning away from that ancient faith.
segments.s698-g86: هم 'also/likewise' links this clause to the previous one. از راه و آیین treats 'راه' as 'way/method' and 'آیین' as 'ritual/custom'; طهمورثی qualifies them as 'of Tahmuras.' The syntax is a simple coordination: 'also from the way and rites of Tahmuras.'
segments.s698-g87: که introduces reported speech/explanation ('that' / 'for they say'). گویند is the classical 3rd-person plural reporting verb 'they say.' داری‍/دارای گیهان 'possessor/owner of the world' (دارای = having/possessing; گیهان = the world). یکی‌ست is the copular statement 'is one' (i.e., there is but one owner).
segments.s698-g88: جز از = 'except from' (جز = except). بندگی کردنت is a verbal-noun + possessive/gerund construction: بندگی 'servitude/devotion' + کردنت 'your doing' (کردنت = 'your doing/being'), i.e. 'your becoming a servant/servitude.' رای نیست is an existential negation 'there is no counsel/choice' (رای = counsel/choice; نیست = is not). Together: 'there is no counsel except that you become a servant.'
segments.s698-g89: A nominal phrase in apposition: جهاندار (world‑ruler) and دهقان یزدان‌پرست (the God‑worshipping dehqān) are listed together; no copula is expressed here, so the whole phrase functions as the subject for the following clause. یزدان‌پرست is a compound adjective/noun meaning “one who worships Yezdan/God.”
segments.s698-g90: چو = 'when'. بر + واژه = 'upon the word/at the utterance' (بر used as a prepositional particle 'on/upon'). برسم is used as 'reach/come to' (poetic present), and بگیرد is a third‑person singular present (subjunctive/optative/poetic present) meaning 'seizes/takes'. به دست is a prepositional phrase 'in/with the hand' (object or manner). Together: 'When he reaches the word, he takes (it) in his hand.'
segments.s698-g91: نشاید is an impersonal present meaning 'it is not fitting / ought not'; it takes the infinitive چشیدن ('to taste') as its complement. یکی قطره is the numeral + noun ('one drop'), with the numeral preceding the noun as is common in classical Persian; آب is the object 'water.'
segments.s698-g92: گر = conditional 'if'. از here means 'because of / from' (indicating cause: 'from thirst'). تشنگی = 'thirst' (reason). آب بیند = 'sees water' (بیند = 'sees' in the present/poetic form). به خواب = adverbial phrase 'in a dream' (به used here in the sense 'in'). The clause reads 'if, because of thirst, he sees water in a dream.'
segments.s698-g93: به یزدان پناهند = 'they take refuge in God' (پناهند is present plural). به‌روز نبرد is a temporal phrase 'on the day of battle' (به‌روز = 'on the day', نبرد = 'battle'), placed after the verb to indicate when.
segments.s698-g94: نخواهد functions here as a negative modal meaning 'will not [want/have]' (poetic/elliptical third person). به جنگ اندرون = 'in/for the inner fighting' (به introduces the noun phrase; اندرون = 'inside/inner quarters'). آب سرد = 'cold water' (noun + adjective; Persian normally places adjective after noun). Together: 'they will not desire cold water for the inner fight.'
segments.s698-g95: همان = 'that very / the same' (demonstrative). قبله‌شان = 'their qibla' (قبله `qibla/object of devotion` with the possessive enclitic -شان). برترین گوهر = 'the most supreme jewel' (superlative adjective برترین before noun گوهر). است = copula 'is'. Word order: demonstrative + possessed noun + superlative adjective + noun + copula.
segments.s698-g96: که = relative 'which/that'. از ... برتر است is the standard comparative pattern 'is superior to ...' (از = 'than/from' in comparison). آب و خاک و هوا lists the things being compared ('water, earth, and air'), and برتر است is the comparative predicate 'is superior.'
segments.s698-g17: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase (چنین 'thus' + گفت 'said') with an omitted explicit subject — 'he/one said thus'. کز is a literary subordinating conjunction (variant of که + از) introducing the reported clause. آ‌هن است آن سوار uses a nominal clause with the copula است placed between predicate and subject (predicate-first order is common in Persian poetry), so literally 'iron is that rider' → 'that rider is made of iron.'
segments.s698-g18: This is a verbless (ellipsis of copula) poetic clause: همه خانه 'the whole house/household' followed by از marking material or composition ('of/from') and the noun phrase گوهر شاهوار 'gems fit for a king' (شاهوار is an adjective meaning 'kingly/regal' attached to گوهر). Together it means 'the entire house was of kingly jewels.'
segments.s698-g19: که introduces the subordinate content. دانا is an epithet 'the wise one' as subject; ورا is an enclitic/old object pronoun meaning 'him' (to him); مغنیاطیس is a loanword meaning 'magnet' or 'magnetism' used here as a name/label; خواند is past tense 'called' (here 'named/called him'). Word order is subject (دانا) — object (ورا مغنیاطیس) — verb (خواند), typical of literary Persian.
segments.s698-g20: The second كـِه continues the report. رومیش 'the Roman' (a person from Rome/by extension a Roman) is the object/affected person; بر + اسپ هندی is a prepositional phrase 'upon an Indian steed' (بر = on; اسپ هندی = 'Indian horse/steed'); نشاند is an archaic/transitive past-tense verb 'sat/placed (someone) (on something)' — verb-final order places the action at the end.
segments.s698-g21: هرآنکس که is a standard conditional/relative 'whoever (that)'. او دفتر هندوان is the noun phrase object of the verb in the next segment: او 'his' (or 'that one’s') + دفتر 'book' + هندوان 'of the Indians/Hindus' (genitive relation). Word order places the object phrase before the verb in the protasis clause.
segments.s698-g22: بخواند is the present/subjunctive or habitual-present form (ب‑ prefix + خواند) used in protasis: 'whoever reads'. شود is the present-tense of شدن 'become' serving as the main verb of the apodosis, and شاد و روشن‌روان are predicative adjectives 'happy and bright‑soul(ed)/cheerful'. The sequence expresses a general consequence: 'whoever reads ... becomes ...'.
segments.s698-g23: بپرسید is past tense 'asked' (verb before subject, a common poetic inversion) and قیصر is the subject 'Caesar'. The conjunction که introduces the indirect question clause; هندی is the subject of that clause 'the Indian', and ز راه is a prepositional phrase 'from the road/from the way' specifying direction.
segments.s698-g24: همی is a literary prefix/adverb signalling ongoing or imminent action before the verb. تا کجا 'how far / to what point' asks the extent, and برکشد is a present/future‑oriented verb (بر + کشد) meaning 'will draw/extend/push (forward)' with پایگاه 'position/base' as its object. The clause therefore asks how far the Indian will advance his base/position along the road.
segments.s698-g73: همان کشته را نیز بر دار کرد is built like Subject/Topic + object marker + verb: همان کشته را ("that same slain [man]" with را marking the definite direct object) + نیز ("also") + بر دار کرد (literally "made on the gallows," an idiomatic way to say "hung"). کرد is simple past (he did/made) describing the completed action.
segments.s698-g74: بر آن دار ("on that gallows") repeats the locative preposition and identifies the place; بر مر ورا خوار کرد is a causative/result clause: بر + pronouns (مر = 'me', ورا = 'him/there') indicates 'in front of me and him/others' and خوار کرد ("made despised/ humiliated") has کرد as the past verb. The sequence says he humiliated the person on that gallows in the sight of the speaker and others.
segments.s698-g75: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; روشن‌روان گشت is a verbal phrase where گشت (past of گشتن) means 'became'—so 'became bright‑soul/cheerful'; و links the predicate to the following adjective. دانش‌پذیر functions adjectivally ('receptive to knowledge' or 'teachable'), describing the state he entered.
segments.s698-g76: A simple coordination of qualities: سخن‌گوی و داننده و یادگیر lists attributes ("eloquent / speaker, knower, and learner"). These are nouns/adjectival nouns that complete the description begun by گشت in the previous segment (i.e., 'he became ... eloquent, knowing, and a learner').
segments.s698-g77: به پیغمبری نیز هنگام یافت uses به + noun to mark attainment ('attained to prophethood'); نیز = 'also/even'; هنگام یافت ('found an occasion/time') uses یافت (past) meaning 'obtained/found'—so the clause says he even came by an opportunity for prophethood. This is an older/poetic phrasing where به marks the goal/status attained.
segments.s698-g78: به برنایی از زیرکی کام یافت is structured as 'به [برنایی] از [زیرکی] کام یافت': کام یافت (literally 'found satisfaction/attained desire') is the main predicate; از زیرکی ('by/from shrewdness') gives the means; به برنایی here marks the person involved ('in/with a youth' or 'to a young man'). The whole means he achieved his desire by/through the youth's cleverness.
segments.s698-g79: تو گویی که is an idiomatic introductory clause meaning 'you would say that' or 'it seems as if'. فرزند یزدان is a genitive phrase 'child/son of God'. The sequence بد اوی is an archaic copular phrase (here equivalent to 'he was'); the form بد is an older/perverted form of the copula and appears in poetry rather than its ordinary modern senses.
segments.s698-g80: بر آن دار برگشته is a locative + participle phrase: بر آن دار 'on that gallows' + برگشته (a participle/past form meaning 'having returned' or 'turned back') describing his posture/appearance; خندان 'smiling' is another adjectival descriptor. Again بد اوی is the archaic past copula ('he was'), so the whole describes him as if he were a divine offspring who had come back to the gallows smiling.
segments.s698-g25: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' attached to the following phrase; دین پرستندگان is a genitive/compound meaning 'worshippers of religion' (noun + follower); برچند is an interrogative adverb 'how many / how much' placed after the phrase to ask about their number/extent.
segments.s698-g26: همه is the universal quantifier 'all/everyone' (subject). بت 'idol' is the object of پرستند (worship); the element گر here introduces the conditional clause (archaic/poetic: گر = 'if'), linking to the following phrase خود کند 'if he himself makes/does it' (کند = 3rd sg. present 'does/makes', خود = 'himself'). The clause reads: 'All worship idols, if he himself makes (it).' (i.e. If someone makes an idol, everyone will worship it.)
segments.s698-g27: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' placed before the reporting verb. گفت is the past reporting verb 'said' (here used with the person-name that follows as the speaker). خراد برزین is the speaker's name (Barzin qualified by خراد, 'the wise' or a proper name element). که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the subordinate clause; راه is the subject of that subordinate clause 'the road'.
segments.s698-g28: به هند اندرون is a locative phrase: به + هند + اندرون = 'within/to India' (poetic ordering places اندرون after هند). گاو شاه is a genitive/compound 'the king's cow' (noun + possessor without overt ezâfe in poetry). است is the copula 'is/are' and و ماه continues the list: 'and the moon.' The clause states existence/containment: 'within India is the king's cow and the moon.'
segments.s698-g29: به + یزدان = 'toward/to the gods/God' (direction/recipient). نگروند here is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'they do not look/turn (toward)' or 'they do not regard' (the verb is used impersonally/plurally). و joins this clause to گردان سپهر, where گردان is a participle/adjective 'revolving' modifying سپهر 'the sky/heaven'.
segments.s698-g30: ندارد is the 3rd singular present 'has not / does not have' (here functioning as a general negative existential about possession). کسی is the indefinite 'anyone' (with the negative it yields 'no one'). بر تن خویش = 'upon his body' (بر = 'on/upon', تن = 'body', خویش split into خوی + ش in the word list but functions as the reflexive/possessive 'his own'); مهر here denotes a 'seal/mark' (rather than merely 'affection'). The whole clause means 'no one has a (seal/mark) upon his body.'
segments.s698-g31: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' used here with a motion verb; خورشید گردنده is a noun phrase 'the turning/rotating sun' (خورشید 'sun' modified by the participle گردنده 'turning'). بر بگذرند is the verb phrase 'they pass over/by' (ب- prefix + بگذرند = 3rd pl. present/subjunctive of 'to pass'); the pair of prepositions ز ... بر with گُذَشت produces the sense 'to pass by/over something.'
segments.s698-g32: چو here is a subordinating particle 'when/as/since' introducing the reason/explanation. ما را uses the object/contrast marker را ('us' as object/emphatic). ز دانندگان = 'from/among the knowers' (ز = 'from', دانندگان = 'those who know/experts'). نشمرند is the 3rd pl. negative/optative form of 'to count/consider' (ن- prefix + شمرند): 'they do not reckon/consider.' Together: 'since/when they do not reckon us among the knowers.'
segments.s698-g49: وگر introduces a conditional (‘if’). برزند is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person plural present subjunctive/indicative (here conditional) meaning ‘come/fall upon’ or ‘strike’; it governs the object کف (‘palm’). به رخسار تو is a prepositional phrase (به + noun + pronoun) meaning ‘onto your cheek/face’ (به = toward/onto; رخسار = cheek/countenance; تو = you).
segments.s698-g50: شود is the 3rd-person singular copular/impersonal ‘become’ (it will become). تیره is adjective ‘dark’. زآن is the prepositional phrase ‘from that/because of that’ pointing back to the preceding blow; زخم دیدار تو is a noun phrase ‘the wound of (the) meeting/seeing you’ (زخم = wound; دیدار = meeting/seeing; تو = your/you), so the whole clause says the face will be darkened because of that wound caused by the sight/meeting of you.
segments.s698-g51: مزن is the negative imperative ‘do not strike’ (مَنفیِ امر). همچنان here means ‘in the same way/like that’ or ‘thus’. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ‘so that / in order that’. بماندت is an archaic 3rd-person subjunctive/optative of ماندن ‘to remain’ (may/let it remain). نام is the thing that should remain (name/reputation).
segments.s698-g52: خردمند is a noun ‘the wise (man)’ used as possessor/relatum. را is the definite/object marker highlighting نام as the object/topic here (the name belonging to the wise man). نام بهتَر sets up the proverb-like statement ‘a name/reputation is better’. ز in this poetic usage functions as the comparative ‘than’. کام = desire/wish; so the clause reads ‘the name of the wise (—) is better than desire.’
segments.s698-g53: بسوتام is a proper name (person/place). را is the object marker marking Basutam as the patient of the following command. بس here functions as ‘enough’/‘stop it’ and with کن (imperative of کردن) forms the admonition ‘make it enough / stop (it)’. از خوردنی is a prepositional phrase ‘from the food/edibles’ (از = from; خوردنی = food/what is to be eaten); together the segment instructs to stop Basutam with respect to food (i.e. give him no more).
segments.s698-g54: مجو is the negative imperative ‘do not seek’. ار is the conditional ‘if’. نباشدت is an archaic negative optative/subjunctive (roughly ‘it should not be / it will not be’). گستردنی is an adjective/participle ‘that can be spread / that will be supplied/extended’ (i.e. made available). Together the clause warns: ‘do not seek (the food) if it will not be spread/provided.’
segments.s698-g55: بدین here (written as ب + دین in the segmentation) functions idiomatically as ‘by this / for this reason / thus’ (a contraction of به + این). سر in such idioms can mean ‘on this account’ or ‘in this matter’. بدی را uses را to mark the object ‘evil/harm’. به بد is the phrase ‘with evil’ (i.e. in return). مشمرید is an archaic/obsolete verb-form; in context it functions as a negative imperative/forbidding ‘do not do / do not repay’ (so the whole segment reads ‘for this reason, do not repay evil with evil’).
segments.s698-g56: بی‌آزار is an adverbial/adjunct meaning ‘harmlessly / without causing harm’. از این تیرگی is a prepositional phrase ‘from/out of this darkness’ (از = from; این = this; تیرگی = darkness/gloom). بگذرید is the 2nd-person plural/polite imperative/subjunctive of گذریدن ‘to pass’ (here: ‘pass through / pass beyond’). The clause therefore commands: ‘pass out of this darkness harmlessly.’
segments.s698-g33: هرآنکس که = a relative/head phrase 'whoever' + complementizer 'که' ('that/who'). او is the personal subject 'he' placed before the verb (poetic prose often allows pronoun subject before verb). آتشی is the object 'a fire' (indefinite/-i form) and برفروخت is a past verb meaning 'kindled' (an older/poetic use rather than 'sold'); the verb ends the clause as is standard in Persian relative clauses.
segments.s698-g34: شد here functions as an auxiliary/inchoative ('came to, ended up') introducing the result clause. اندر میان = locative prepositional phrase 'in/within the midst (of)'. خویشتن را is the reflexive object 'himself' marked by را (definite/object marker). بسوخت is the main past verb 'burned/was burned' — together شد ... بسوخت expresses 'came to burn [himself]'.
segments.s698-g35: یکی = 'one' / 'someone' introducing an individual. آتشی is the thing known ('a fire'); داند is the present/habitual verb 'knows' (classical present used narratively). اندر هوا is a locative 'in the air/sky'; word order places subject/indefinite noun before object and the verb at the end as typical.
segments.s698-g36: به فرمان = instrumental/agentive 'by the command (of)'. یزدان is the divine agent 'God' and فرمان‌روا is an epithet in apposition 'the sovereign/ruler' (a title attached to یزدان). به here marks agency/instrument rather than motion.
segments.s698-g37: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding notion ('that/which'). دانای هندوش = 'the Indian sage' (subject + epithet). خواند here means 'called/named' (he gave a name) and اثیر is the name/term attached (object of خواند). The verb appears at the end of the clause, as standard.
segments.s698-g38: سخنهای نغز = 'pithy/delicate words' (supply of words marked by plural suffix). آورد is the past tense 'he brought/forth produced' and دلپذیر is an attributive/predicative adjective 'pleasant' describing the words — together 'he produced delightful words.' Word order places the noun phrase before the verb, then the qualifying adjective.
segments.s698-g39: چنین گفت = formulaic 'thus said (he)' introducing speech. کآتش is 'that fire' (کـ = relative/ demonstrative-prefix + آتش) and به آتش رسید = 'reached/reached to the fire' (رسید past 'reached/arrived'); the clause is a reported statement introduced by چنين گفت and the verb is final.
segments.s698-g40: گناهش = 'his sin' (possessed object). ز کردار = causal prepositional phrase 'from/because of his deeds' (ز here marks cause). شد ناپدید is a predicative phrase 'became vanished/gone' (شد = 'became', ناپدید = 'vanished'), so the whole means 'his sin, because of his deeds, became gone.'
segments.s698-g57: شما را هوا بر خرد شاه گشت is structured as 'شما را + هوا + بر + خرد شاه + گشت'. Here شما را (with را marking the object) means 'a desire for you'; هوا is a noun 'desire/passion' and بر + خرد شاه 'upon the king's wisdom/judgment'. گشت is the past-tense verb 'became/occurred' used impersonally: 'a desire came upon the king's reason'. The word order is typical of classical/poetic Persian where the thing desired (شما را) appears up front and the light verb گشت concludes the clause.
segments.s698-g58: دل از آز بسیار بیراه گشت literally places the subject دل 'heart' up front, then gives the reason with از + آز ('because of greed'). بسیار modifies آز ('very great greed'). بیراه 'astray/wrong' is the predicate adjective and گشت 'became' is the past copula: 'the heart became astray because of great greed.' The preposition از is used here in its causal sense ('because of').
segments.s698-g59: که introduces a subordinate/time clause here (classical usage 'when/that'). ایوانهاتان is ایوان 'portico/veranda' plus the plural/possessive suffix -ها + -تان 'your'. به + کیوان expresses the astrological/locational relation 'to/under Kayvân (Saturn)'. رسید is the past tense 'reached/arrived' so the clause means 'when your porticoes reached Kayvân.'
segments.s698-g60: شماری is a noun 'a number/reckoning' (here 'a tally/sum'); که شد is a relative clause 'which became'. گنجتان را uses را as the object/topic marker with 'your treasure', and کلید is the predicate 'key'. The poetic order inverts what would be plain prose ('شماری شد کلیدِ گنجتان'): 'a count/number that became the key of your treasure.'
segments.s698-g61: ابا here denotes unwillingness/reluctance (classical usage 'to be unwilling' or in context an admonition 'do not be reluctant / do not withhold'). گنجتان is 'your treasure' (possessive), نیز is 'also/likewise', چندان is a quantifier 'so much/such', and سپاه is 'an army'. Together the phrase admonishes against withholding treasure and introduces that even so great an army is at issue.
segments.s698-g62: زره‌های رومی و رومی کلاه lists military equipment: زره‌های (plural 'armors/cuirasses') + رومی 'Byzantine/Roman'. The conjunction و 'and' links to رومی کلاه, where the adjective precedes the noun for poetic inversion (more usual prose order would be کلاه رومی). The whole phrase means 'Byzantine armors and Byzantine helmets.'
segments.s698-g63: به هر جای بیداد is a prepositional phrase 'to/at every place of oppression' (به ... جای + noun). لشکر کشید is the verbal compound 'لشکر کشیدن' meaning 'to march/send an army'; کشید here is the past tense 'marched/sent'. The clause literally reads 'to every place of oppression an army was sent/marched.'
segments.s698-g64: ز آسودگی uses poetic ز (short form of از) meaning 'from/because of' and آسودگی 'ease/relief/tranquility'. تیغها is the plural subject 'the swords' and برکشید is the past 'drew up/raised' (here 'drew [their] swords'). The order places the causal phrase first: 'from ease the swords were drawn.'
segments.s698-g65: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle here ('indeed'/'continually') placed before the verb. چشمه (spring) is the predicate noun and گردد (becomes) is the present/past-form verb 'become' placed after it in poetic inversion: 'the desert becomes a spring (of) blood'. ز marks the source/constituent 'from' (i.e. 'a spring of blood').
segments.s698-g66: نبود is the past negative copula 'there was not / was not'. مسیحا is the noun 'Messiah' (not vocative here) and اندر این رهنمون is a prepositional phrase 'in/within this guide/guidance' with اندر='in'. Word order is Subject (مسیحا) + Verb (نبود) + locative phrase (اندر این رهنمون).
segments.s698-g67: یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain' placed before the description. بینوا modifies مرد (a poor man) and درویش further identifies him as a dervish; بود is the past copula 'was'. The phrase is a simple nominal clause: 'There was a certain poor man, a dervish.'
segments.s698-g68: که introduces a relative clause modifying that man ('who/whose...'). نانش is نان + enclitic -ش 'his bread' used idiomatically for 'his livelihood'. ز marks the source 'from' and رنج تن = 'the toil/suffering of the body' (رنج 'hardship', تن 'body'). خوی + ش is split in the text: خوی here is part of the reflexive/possessive construction and ش is the enclitic 'his'; بود closes the clause as the past copula 'was'.
segments.s698-g69: جز از ... نبودی ... is an exclusion construction: 'there was no ... except ...'. Here جز از ترف و شیرش introduces the only things he had, and نبودی is the archaic negative past 'there was not'. خورش ('stew/cooked dish') is the noun negated — word order places the negated noun after the verb, a feature found in poetic/classical prose.
segments.s698-g70: فزونیش = فزون + -ش 'its/that one's excess' (the possessive -ش refers back to the items just mentioned). This phrase lacks an explicit verbal copula and reads as a nominal comment: 'its excess (فزونیش) ... بدی پرورش' — 'the excess is/was bad for upbringing'. رخبین and بدی and پرورش form the rest: رخبین is a noun here, بدی 'badness/evil' qualifies پرورش 'upbringing/development'. Classical Persian often omits a linking verb, understood from context.
segments.s698-g71: چو here means 'when'. آورد is the past of آوردن 'brought/struck' in the idiom به مشت آوردن = 'to strike with the fist' (به marks the instrument). مرد is the subject; جهودش is 'his Jew' (جهود used historically for 'Jew') with the possessive -ش attached. The clause means 'When the man struck his Jew with a blow/fist...'.
segments.s698-g72: The second چو again 'when/as' introduces the consequence: 'when he saw him friendless and helpless (بی‌یار و بیچاره), دیدش is دید + enclitic -ش 'he saw him', and بکشت is an archaic past form meaning 'he slew/killed him' (classical compound past form). Word order is prototypical: temporal conjunction + condition + perception + action.
segments.s698-g41: This is a causal prepositional phrase: از آن (’because of that’) gives the cause, followed by ناگزیر (an adjectival/adverbial word meaning ‘inevitable/compelled’) which modifies the following verbal noun افروختن (‘to kindle’). The infinitive/masdar افروختن stands as a nominalized action (‘kindling a fire’), so the whole segment reads ‘because of that, inevitably kindling a fire.’
segments.s698-g42: This clause contains the finite verb خواند (past, 3sg) used in the sense ‘called/named.’ The object (همان راستی, ‘that very truth’) comes before the verb and the nominative/subject-like phrase (این سوختن, ‘this burning’) follows the verb — a common poetic/inverted order meaning ‘he called this burning that very truth.’
segments.s698-g43: A short predicative clause: همان گفت‌وگوی شما is the topical NP (‘that conversation of yours’), followed by the negative copula نیست (‘is not’). The final راست is an adverbial/predicative intensifier meaning ‘true/really’ (here negated by نیست), so the segment reads ‘that conversation of yours is not true.’
segments.s698-g44: This is a locative/agentive witness clause: بر این (‘to/regarding this’) and بر روان مسیحا (‘upon the soul/spirit of the Messiah’) are prepositional phrases framing the statement, and گواست is a contracted/poetic predicate meaning ‘is witness’ or ‘bears witness’ (an archaic form akin to گواه است). The word order is emphatic/poetic, placing the witness phrase after the topicalizing prepositional phrase.
segments.s698-g45: نبینی is a negative interrogative/present form meaning ‘do you not see’ (a rhetorical question). The subordinate clause introduced by که gives its content: عیسی مریم is the classical appositional name ‘Jesus [of] Mary’ (noun + mother’s name), and چه گفت is ‘what he said’ (چه as interrogative object, گفت intended as past ‘said’).
segments.s698-g46: بدانگه (‘thereupon/at that time’) is an adverbial linking word; که again introduces a content clause. بگشاد is an older/poetic past verb form meaning ‘opened/revealed’ (the b‑ prefix is an archaic/perfective marker), راز is the direct object ‘secret,’ and از نهفت is a prepositional phrase ‘from concealment’ (راز از نهفت = ‘the secret from hiding’ = ‘the hidden secret’).
segments.s698-g47: This is a conditional protasis introduced by که here functioning like ‘if’: پیراهنت‌گر is a compact/poetic conditional compound (پیراهن + -ت + گر) meaning roughly ‘if your shirt’ or ‘if someone takes your shirt’ and ستاند is the past of ستادن (in older Persian, ‘to take away/snatch’). کسی is the indefinite ‘someone.’ Together the segment means ‘if someone takes your shirt.’
segments.s698-g48: میاویز is a present/imperfective verb meaning ‘hang on/cling’ (imperative or hortative sense here). با او (‘with him’) marks the target, and به تندی بسی is an adverbial phrase: به + تندی (‘with fierceness/violence’) plus بسی (‘very/much’ in poetic use) intensifies the action. The whole says ‘cling to him very fiercely.’
segments.s698-g1: بدو (a clitic prepositional phrase) precedes the verb and means 'to him' (dative). گفت is the past tense 'said' but the provided senses list does not include the verb sense (so its sense is marked -1); قیصر is the subject 'the Caesar' placed after the verb (typical flexible word order in classical Persian). که introduces the reported speech and جاوید زی is an archaic imperative phrase: جاوید 'eternal' + زی (imperative 'live') = 'live forever.'
segments.s698-g2: که begins a subordinate clause giving the content of the command. دستور شاهنشهان را is a definite NP with the object marker را after شاهنشهان ('the ordinance/order of the emperors'). The final word سزی is an archaic/poetic verbal form (here functioning as the main verb of the clause, roughly 'do/arrange' or 'befit'), so the clause means 'that you arrange/establish the emperor's ordinance'; the exact lexical sense of سزی is unclear in the supplied list and thus marked -1.
segments.s698-g3: یکی is an indefinite/quantifying word 'a certain' placed before خانه 'house.' دارم is the 1st-person present 'I have' and follows the NP (common S–O–V flexibility in Persian). در ایوان شگفت is a locative prepositional phrase 'in a wondrous iwan/porch' modifying خانه.
segments.s698-g4: کزین = 'from this/than this' introduces a comparative/limiting phrase; برتر اندازه = 'a greater/superior measure' (برتر modifying اندازه). نتوان گرفت is a negative potential construction: نتوان (cannot) + گرفت (past stem of گرفتن) yields 'cannot be taken/one cannot take,' so together 'no greater measure can be taken.' The past stem گرفت is used after توانستن to form inability (classical pattern).
segments.s698-g5: یکی اسب و مردی is an instruction 'one horse and a man' (indefinite/quantifying one + nouns). برو is an imperative 'go' (or 'ride/ride off' in context), and بر سوار ('on [a] steed' or 'onto the rider/horse') indicates where/to what to go — the phrase functions as an imperatival direction to mount/take the horse with its rider. Word order is compressed for poetic imperatives.
segments.s698-g6: کز introduces a source clause 'from/whence'; آنجا 'there' is the location. شگفتی is a noun 'a marvel/wonder' and serves as the subject of شود ('will happen/become' — classical present/future sense). هوشیار is a predicative adjective 'alert/on guard' linked to the clause (imperative or exhortation sense: be alert because a wonder will occur).
segments.s698-g7: چو here is conditional 'if/when' and is followed by بینی 'you see' (present second person). ندانی is the negated present 'you do not know' (ن- negative prefix + دانى). که introduces the embedded interrogative/nominal clause 'این بند چیست' ('what this stanza is'), where the copula ست appears as ست attached to چی (چیست in inverted poetic order).
segments.s698-g8: طلسم names the thing: 'a talisman/enchanted spell.' است‌گر is the classical conditional sequence (است + گر) meaning 'if it is/should it be'; کرده is the perfect participle 'made/wrought.' ایزدی‌ست is a final predicate 'it is divine/of God' (ایزدی = 'divine, of God' + ست copula). Together: 'it is a talisman; if made, it is of divine making.'
segments.s698-g97: نباشند is a negative optative/subjunctive third-person plural of بودن (with the negative prefix ن-), functioning like “may they not be.” شاهان ما follows and is the subject phrase: شاهان (kings) + enclitic ما = “our kings.” دین‌فروش is a compound noun “religion‑seller” (i.e. a religious charlatan); it is the predicate of the verb.
segments.s698-g98: Construction: به فرمانِ دارنده دارند گوش = literally “they have an ear to the command of the holder.” دارند is the present plural “they have” (here part of the idiom گوش داشتن = to pay attention/heed). به + فرمان marks the object of attention (“to the command”), and دارنده is a noun modifying فرمان (“the holder/possessor,” i.e. the one in authority). Overall: “they heed the command of the ruler.”
segments.s698-g99: به + noun phrase (دینار و گوهر) is an adverbial/causal phrase here: “because of / for dinars and jewels.” نباشند is again the negative optative/subjunctive “may they not be,” and شاد is the predicate adjective “happy, glad.” Word order places the prepositional/causal phrase before the verb and adjective.
segments.s698-g100: نجویند is a subjunctive/optative plural from جستن/جَویدن in the sense “seek/search (for)” — here “may they not seek.” نام و نشان is a fixed collocation meaning “name and mark” = reputation/renown. جز به داد is a restrictive phrase “except by/through justice” (جز = except; به داد = by means of justice). The verb expresses wish/command consistent with the previous clauses.
segments.s698-g101: ببخشیدن is an infinitival/masdar form meaning “to give (away)” or “to grant” here (used as one item in a list of good deeds). کاخ‌های is the plural possessive/construct (“palaces”) and بلند is the adjective “tall/lofty,” following it. The object follows the verb form in this verbless/infinite clause pattern.
segments.s698-g102: دگر is an adverb here meaning “again / furthermore.” شاد کردن is a causative/light‑verb construction (شاد + کردن) meaning “to make happy,” with کردن functioning as the light verb “to cause / make.” دل مستمند = “the heart of the needy” (دل = heart, مستمند = needy/poor) — i.e. “to gladden the needy.”
segments.s698-g103: سدیگر functions as a transitional/distributive particle — “and another / furthermore.” کسی کو is an indefinite + relative construction (کسی … کو) meaning “anyone who / someone who”; classical Persian often uses کو as a relative/indefinite marker. به‌روز is adverbial “on the day / by day.” نبرد here is a negative/optative/indicative form related to بردن (“to take/go”), used in a negative-wish sense (“does not go / would not go / should not go”); the clause as a whole rules out leaving someone dishonored on the day in question.
segments.s698-g104: بپوشد is a subjunctive/imperative‑like third‑person singular of پوشیدن with the prefix ب‑ (“let him/her/it cover”); here it means “let (one) cover/conceal.” رخ is “face.” شید is used as “beloved” (the beloved’s face), and گردان is a participial/adjectival sense “turning / revolving” (here “turned aside / turned about”); به گرد is a prepositional phrase “around / about.” Together the phrase says: do not veil/conceal the beloved’s face and turn it away.

sentences.s698-s5: When Kharād Barzīn heard these words, he came to that ancient seat.
sentences.s698-s6: He saw a certain high-made seat; in it stood a noble rider.
sentences.s698-s7: Nowhere had the eye of the beholder seen the like; you said, 'God created (him) in that manner.'
sentences.s698-s8: He saw a suspended, standing rider; he came up to the famed Caesar.
sentences.s698-s53: The wise king wants nothing except this: that the enemy keep their gaze off his homeland.
sentences.s698-s54: Whoever seeks anything of religion other than truth — may the unpraiseworthy curse be upon him.
sentences.s698-s55: When the Caesar heard it he was pleased; his words seemed useful to him.
sentences.s698-s56: He said to him: 'He who created the world made you renowned among the great.'
sentences.s698-s57: One must hear pure words from you; you possess the key to the secrets.
sentences.s698-s58: There was no one of this sort who was inferior; his head rose above the moon's diadem.
sentences.s698-s59: He asked for dirhams, he asked for treasure and dinars; he asked for a certain renowned diadem.
sentences.s698-s60: He gave it to him and praised him much, saying, 'May the land of Iran be prosperous because of you.'
sentences.s698-s41: A wise man laughs at this; if you are wise yourself, do not meddle in this art.
sentences.s698-s42: For he is without need of children or a wife, and the secret is manifest near him.
sentences.s698-s43: What a departure from the faith of Keyumars, and likewise from the ways and rites of Tahmuras.
sentences.s698-s44: They say the possessor of the world is one; there is no counsel except to become a servant.
sentences.s698-s45: When the God‑worshipping dehqān (or the world's lord) comes upon the word, he takes it in his hand.
sentences.s698-s46: It is not fitting to taste even a single drop of water if, from thirst, one sees water in a dream.
sentences.s698-s47: They take refuge in God; on the day of battle they will not desire cold water in the inner fighting.
sentences.s698-s48: That very qibla of theirs is the supreme jewel, which is superior to water, earth, and air.
sentences.s698-s9: Thus he said that that rider was made of iron; his whole house was of kingly jewels.
sentences.s698-s10: That the wise man called him 'magnet' and set the Roman on an Indian steed.
sentences.s698-s11: Whoever reads his book of the Indians will become joyful and light‑souled.
sentences.s698-s12: Caesar asked, 'From the road, how far will the Indian push forward his position?'
sentences.s698-s37: He also hanged that same slain man, and on that gallows he made him a figure of contempt before me and others.
sentences.s698-s38: When he became bright‑spirited and receptive to knowledge — eloquent, knowing, and a learner,
sentences.s698-s39: He even found occasion for prophethood; through a youth's shrewdness he attained his desire.
sentences.s698-s40: You would say he was a child of God — he had returned to that gallows, smiling he was.
sentences.s698-s13: How many are the worshippers of religion? If he makes it himself, everyone will worship an idol.
sentences.s698-s14: Thus said Khurad Barzin that the road to India contains within it the king's cow and the moon.
sentences.s698-s15: They do not turn to the gods, and the revolving heaven bears no seal upon anyone's body.
sentences.s698-s16: They pass by the revolving sun, for they do not reckon us among the knowers.
sentences.s698-s25: And if they strike your cheek with a palm, it will be darkened from that wound — the wound of having seen you.
sentences.s698-s26: Do not strike like that, so that the wise man’s name may remain — a good name is better than desire.
sentences.s698-s27: Give Basutam no more (food); stop him from eating — do not seek (food) if it will not be made available.
sentences.s698-s28: For this reason do not repay evil with evil; pass out of this darkness harmlessly.
sentences.s698-s17: Whoever kindled a fire ended up burning himself within his own breast.
sentences.s698-s18: There is one who knows of a fire in the air, by the command of God the sovereign.
sentences.s698-s19: which the Indian sage called 'athir'; he brought forth delicate, pleasing words.
sentences.s698-s20: Thus he said: that fire reached fire; his sin, by his deeds, became vanished.
sentences.s698-s29: A passion for you came upon the king's reason; his heart, because of excessive greed, went astray.
sentences.s698-s30: When your porticoes came under Kayvân (Saturn), a reckoning — which became the key to your treasure — was made.
sentences.s698-s31: Do not hold back your treasure; likewise, so great an army — Byzantine cuirasses and Byzantine helmets.
sentences.s698-s32: Wherever there was oppression, an army marched forth; from their ease the swords were drawn.
sentences.s698-s33: The wilderness will indeed turn into a spring of blood; there is no Messiah in this guidance.
sentences.s698-s34: There was a certain poor man, a dervish, whose bread (livelihood) came from the toil of his own body.
sentences.s698-s35: He had no cooked dish except delicacies and his milk; their excess was bad for upbringing.
sentences.s698-s36: When the man struck his Jew with his fist, and when he saw him friendless and helpless, he killed him.
sentences.s698-s21: Because of that, inevitably kindling a fire, he called this burning the very truth.
sentences.s698-s22: That conversation of yours is not true; indeed, the Messiah’s spirit is witness to this.
sentences.s698-s23: Do you not see what Jesus, son of Mary, said thereupon when he revealed the hidden secret?
sentences.s698-s24: If someone were to take your shirt, cling to him very fiercely.
sentences.s698-s1: The Caesar said to him, "Live forever; that you (should) set/arrange the ordinance of the shahanshahs."
sentences.s698-s2: I have a certain house in a marvelous iwan, of such extent that no greater measure can be taken from it.
sentences.s698-s3: Take one horse and a man — go, mount him; from there a wonder will occur — be on your guard.
sentences.s698-s4: If when you see it you do not know what this stanza is, it is a talisman; if it was wrought, it is of divine making.
sentences.s698-s49: May our kings not be religious charlatans; may they heed the commands of the one in authority.
sentences.s698-s50: Let them not be glad for dinars and jewels; let them not seek name and token except through justice.
sentences.s698-s51: To give away lofty palaces, and again to make the needy’s heart glad.
sentences.s698-s52: And let there be no other person who, on the day, goes away dishonored; do not let the beloved’s face be veiled and turned aside.
